articles.title: How Did People Find Out About the Declaration of Independence in 1776?
articles.summary: Imagine finding out your country just declared independence — but there's no internet, no TV, no phones! That's what happened in 1776. A new book called 'When the Declaration of Independence Was News' tells how news spread back then. Printers like John Dunlap made posters called broadsides. Newspapers copied the text. People gathered at courthouses and churches for public readings. Some people cheered, but others were worried or unhappy. The famous signed parchment we see today wasn't even ready until August. By then, most people already knew!

articles.title: Tennis Stars Alcaraz and Djokovic Pull Out of Madrid Open Due to Injuries
articles.summary: Two of the world's top tennis players, Carlos Alcaraz and Novak Djokovic, have withdrawn from the Madrid Open next week because of injuries. Alcaraz, 22, has a wrist problem and also missed the Barcelona Open. He said it was 'incredibly difficult' to miss Madrid, which is his home tournament. Djokovic, 38, hasn't played since March due to a shoulder injury. Both are racing to be fit for the French Open, which starts on May 18. Alcaraz has won the French Open the last two years and wants to make it three in a row.

articles.title: You Can Actually Go Inside This Volcano in Iceland!
articles.summary: In Iceland, there's a volcano called Thríhnúkagígur where you can take an elevator down into an empty magma chamber — the place where molten rock used to be! It's the only place on Earth where people can do this. The chamber is 700 feet deep (taller than the Statue of Liberty). The walls are colorful: bronze, indigo, yellow, and orange. Scientists aren't sure why the magma disappeared. They think it might have drained back down into the Earth. Visitors go down in a metal cage and can walk around on the cave floor. Pretty cool, right?
